Amended MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-18-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Dean Royer, Esq. (650) 425-6264 SZETO-WONG LAW for Plaintiff.
Denae M. Olivieri, Esq. 415.433.0990 WILSON, ELSER, MOSKOWITZ, EDELMAN & DICKER LLP
Court Reporter: Jaime Ply, CSR 13323, 714.316.3757, jplycsr@aol.com

ARGUED. Ms. Olivieri argues. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT SREE VAIDYANATHAN's Amended MOTION TO STRIKE Amended COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendant Sree Vaidyanathan's motion to strike the prayer for punitive damages from the first amended complaint is denied. Plaintiffs adequately allege ultimate facts sufficient to show malice by "despicable conduct ... carried on ... with a willful and conscious disregard of ... the safety of" plaintiffs within the meaning of Civil Code 3294(c)(1).

Order is signed.

Visiting Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/HEK)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-12-2024 Continued To Mar-12-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-22-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Hearing re: DEFENDANT ARDEN OWNERS ASSOCIATION'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling:

Arden Owners Association's motion to strike is granted with 20 days leave to amend as to the claim for punitive damages. The motion is granted without leave to amend as to the claim for attorney's fees as conceded.

Plaintiffs fail to plead sufficient facts showing malice, oppression, or fraud to support the claim for punitive damages. (Civil Code section 3294(a).)

"[M]alice does not require actual intent to harm. [Citation.] Conscious disregard for the safety of another may be sufficient where the defendant is aware of the probable dangerous consequences of his or her conduct and he or she willfully fails to avoid such consequences." (Pfeifer v. John Crane, Inc. (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1270, 1299; see also College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 713 ["For plaintiffs attempting to prove malice by showing a 'conscious disregard' of their rights as opposed to an actual intent to harm, the Act imposed additional requirements of 'despicable' and 'willful' defense conduct.")

Here, plaintiffs allege that movant conducted a background check, knew that co-defendant
Salamanca was previously convicted of assault, and failed to provide a safe environment.
(Complaint, pars. 13, 44-45.) But simply being convicted of an assault does not necessarily
indicate an innate propensity for violence. Plaintiffs should allege facts showing such a
propensity and probable violence. For example, as to the unit owners, plaintiffs allege that they were previously aware of Salamanca's violent conduct on the premises. (Complaint, par. 14.) No such allegations are made against movant. Plaintiffs also allege that defendant did not cooperate with him when he attempted to obtain a restraining order and failed to notify others of the alleged attack. (Complaint, pars. 37-38.) Those allegations, however, are unrelated to the underlying alleged torts.

Reading the complaint liberally per CCP 452, plaintiffs must allege more facts showing a malicious or despicable conscious disregard for plaintiff's safety.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Not Reported. Bailiff: C. Loke. (301/RCE)
